extendedDescription: Komodo sits at the meeting point of the Indian and Pacific Oceans, and the cold, nutrient-rich upwellings that funnel between its islands feed an extraordinary food chain — from coral gardens dense with reef fish to manta cleaning stations, sharks, dolphins and the occasional pelagic. The north of the park is warm, blue and current-driven; the south is cooler, greener and built for mantas and macro. Most divers visit by liveaboard, looping both halves over a week.
seasonNotes: April–June and September–November are the sweet spots: calm seas, warm water (27–29°C) and visibility often above 25m in the north. July–August brings cooler southern water (22–24°C), thicker plankton, and the highest manta odds. The park stays divable year-round but January–March sees stronger wind and rain.
tripDuration: Most divers come for 6–10 nights on a liveaboard, or 4–7 nights land-based out of Labuan Bajo with day trips to the nearer central sites.
diveStyle: Drift diving in moderate-to-ripping current is the norm. Liveaboards dominate because the best sites are spread across both ends of the park; expect 3–4 dives a day, negative entries, and reef hooks at sites like Castle Rock and Crystal Rock.
diveLevel: Advanced Open Water with 30+ logged dives is the realistic floor — operators will let Open Water divers join the calmer central sites, but the marquee dives (Batu Bolong, Castle Rock, Shotgun, Manta Alley) all involve current and depth. Comfort with drift and negative entries matters more than certification cards.

quotes.text: The current at Castle Rock pinned us flat to the reef while jacks tornadoed overhead and grey reefs hunted them — it's the closest thing to a nature documentary I've ever been inside.
Two dives a day for a week and I still felt like we'd only scratched the surface. The north is electric, the south is weird and quiet and full of mantas. Both deserve their own trip.
Komodo isn't the prettiest reef I've dived — Raja wins that — but it's the most alive. Every dive felt like something was about to happen.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly into Labuan Bajo (LBJ) on Flores via Bali or Jakarta. Liveaboards depart from the harbor; land-based ops are 5–15 minutes from the airport.

goodToKnow.title: Park fees
goodToKnow.body: A daily park fee (~IDR 200–300k) plus dive permits are required and usually bundled into operator pricing. Confirm in writing — fees have been restructured several times.

goodToKnow.title: Dragons
goodToKnow.body: Most trips include a half-day on Rinca or Komodo island to see the dragons. Rinca is closer and less touristed; both require a ranger.

goodToKnow.title: Currents are no joke
goodToKnow.body: Carry an SMB and ideally a Nautilus Lifeline or PLB on every dive. Drift separations happen and the channels run fast.

goodToKnow.title: Water temp swings
goodToKnow.body: North-to-south transits in a single day can drop water temp 5°C+. Pack a 5mm or a 3mm + hooded vest rather than a thin shorty.

goodToKnow.title: Surface interval reality
goodToKnow.body: There is no decompression chamber inside the park; the nearest is in Bali. Dive conservatively and consider DAN coverage.

extendedDescription: Raja Ampat sits at the heart of the Coral Triangle and holds the highest recorded marine biodiversity on Earth — more reef fish species than anywhere else, healthy shark and ray populations, and reefs that look untouched because, for most of their history, they were. The four main island groups (Waigeo, Batanta, Salawati, Misool) each have their own character; most trips combine at least two by liveaboard.
seasonNotes: October–April is the main season: glassy seas, 25–30m viz, and the migratory pelagics layered over already-loaded reefs. May–September is the southeast monsoon — Misool gets choppy but the north stays divable, and prices drop sharply.
tripDuration: 10–11 nights is the standard liveaboard itinerary; 7-night trips cover one region (north or Misool). Land-based stays at Sorido, Papua Paradise or Misool Resort run 7–10 nights.
diveStyle: Mostly drift on healthy reef and seamounts, with a handful of muck and mandarin-fish dives mixed in. Currents are usually manageable but Cape Kri and Blue Magic can rip.
diveLevel: Advanced Open Water with 50+ dives is recommended; some operators require it for Misool sites. Buoyancy matters more than depth — the reefs are shallow but fragile.

quotes.text: I've dived 40 countries and Raja is the only place I've surfaced from a dive and immediately wanted to roll back in.
Cape Kri broke the species-per-dive record for a reason — there's literally not a square meter without something on it.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly to Sorong (SOQ) via Jakarta or Manado, then ferry or speedboat to Waisai. Allow a buffer day — internal flights are routinely delayed.

goodToKnow.title: Marine park fee
goodToKnow.body: A one-time tag (~IDR 1m / ~$65) is mandatory and goes to local communities. Keep it on you.

goodToKnow.title: Cash is king
goodToKnow.body: ATMs in Waisai are unreliable. Bring USD or IDR cash for tips, fees and incidentals.

goodToKnow.title: Malaria
goodToKnow.body: Raja is in a malaria zone. Talk to a travel clinic — most divers take prophylaxis.

extendedDescription: A UNESCO marine park 150km offshore in the middle of the Sulu Sea, Tubbataha is reachable only by liveaboard during a tight 3-month window. Two atolls and a small reef bracket pristine walls that drop into deep blue, with reliable encounters with grey reef sharks, jacks, tuna and the occasional whale shark, hammerhead or thresher.
seasonNotes: Mid-March to mid-June only — the rest of the year the park is closed and the seas are unworkable. April–May is the sweet spot: flattest crossings, warmest water, and the highest pelagic odds.
tripDuration: 6–7 nights round-trip from Puerto Princesa, Palawan. There is no day-boat option.
diveStyle: Wall and drift diving with negative entries; 3–4 dives a day plus optional night dives at the ranger station. Currents are moderate but unpredictable.
diveLevel: Advanced Open Water and 50+ dives is the realistic floor; most operators require it. Deep, blue-water comfort matters.

quotes.text: The walls at Tubbataha are what every Caribbean wall wishes it was — twice as alive, half as touched.
We had a thresher cross under us at Shark Airport and the whole boat lost its mind at dinner. That's a normal day there.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Manila → Puerto Princesa (PPS). Boats depart from the harbor; book 6–12 months ahead — the season sells out.

goodToKnow.title: Park fee
goodToKnow.body: ~PHP 18,000 (~$320) conservation fee, usually added on top of the trip price.

goodToKnow.title: Weather risk
goodToKnow.body: The Sulu Sea crossing is the variable. A bad squall can cancel a day; build flexibility into return flights.

extendedDescription: Sipadan is a single oceanic seamount rising 600m from the Celebes Sea floor — a tiny island ringed by walls, turtles, and one of the most reliable barracuda tornados in the sport. Access is capped at 176 divers per day; you stay on Mabul or Kapalai and boat over.
seasonNotes: Divable year-round, but April–December has the calmest seas and clearest water. July–September is peak for big schools.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights based on Mabul or Kapalai. Sipadan permits are issued by day, so longer stays buy more island days.
diveStyle: Wall and drift diving with shallow plateaus; turtles are constant, and Barracuda Point delivers the famous tornado on most dives.
diveLevel: Open Water is fine for most sites; Advanced helps for the deeper wall and any current. Buoyancy matters — the walls drop fast.

quotes.text: I've been diving 25 years and Barracuda Point is still the only site where I just stop moving and watch.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly KL → Tawau, then 1.5h transfer to Semporna and boat to Mabul/Kapalai.

goodToKnow.title: Permit cap
goodToKnow.body: Only 176 divers/day land on Sipadan. Resorts ration permits across guests; book early and confirm permit days in writing.

goodToKnow.title: Safety advisory
goodToKnow.body: Check current government travel advisories for east Sabah before booking.

extendedDescription: A black-sand fishing village on Bali's northeast coast, Tulamben is built around the USAT Liberty — a 120m WWII wreck that sits 30m from the beach in 5–30m of water. The wreck is famous, but the macro on the surrounding muck and the drop-off at the next bay over make it a full week's diving without a boat.
seasonNotes: April–November is dry season with the calmest entries and best viz (15–25m). December–March is wetter with occasional surge, but the diving still happens.
tripDuration: 3–5 nights is typical; macro photographers and tech divers regularly stay 7–10.
diveStyle: Shore diving from the cobble beach — guides porter tanks for a small fee. Easy current, gentle depths, photographer-friendly.
diveLevel: Open Water is fine; the Liberty's shallow sections are accessible to snorkelers. Advanced helps to explore the deeper bow and the Drop-Off.

quotes.text: Nowhere else can I do four shore dives a day on a world-class wreck and walk back to a warung for nasi goreng between them.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: ~3h drive from Denpasar (DPS). Pre-arrange transfer; taxis are scarce on the north coast.

goodToKnow.title: Early-morning crowds
goodToKnow.body: Liberty gets busy by 9am with day-trippers. Stay locally and dive it at sunrise — it's a different wreck.

goodToKnow.title: Cobble entry
goodToKnow.body: Booties with stiff soles save ankles. The walk in is the hardest part of the dive.

extendedDescription: A 48km-long limestone island off the Yucatán, Cozumel is the easiest first-class drift diving in the western hemisphere: clear blue water pushed north along a chain of cuts, swim-throughs, and coral spurs that bottom out at 20–40m. Big eagle ray and turtle traffic, splendid toadfish on the sand, and a reef that has bounced back well from past bleaching.
seasonNotes: May–September is warm (28–29°C) and calm. November–March brings cooler water and stronger north winds that can close sites. June–August is hurricane season — book flexible.
tripDuration: 4–7 nights; dive ops run two-tank morning trips and afternoon shore dives.
diveStyle: All drift, all the time. Boats follow the bubbles and pick you up downstream. Most sites are 12–25m; deeper sites available.
diveLevel: Open Water with comfort drifting; Advanced unlocks the deeper swim-throughs at Palancar and Punta Sur.

quotes.text: I learned to dive in Cozumel and now I judge every drift dive against it. Most lose.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly CZM direct, or PCM/CUN + ferry from Playa del Carmen (45 min).

goodToKnow.title: Marine park fee
goodToKnow.body: ~$5/day, usually collected by your op. Keep your wristband.

goodToKnow.title: Hurricane season
goodToKnow.body: August–October peak risk; trip insurance with weather coverage is sensible.

extendedDescription: Darwin is the northernmost island in the Galápagos archipelago and one of the planet's elite shark sites — schooling hammerheads, Galápagos sharks, silkies and whale sharks (in season) move along a single submerged arch. There's no land diving here; access is liveaboard-only on a tightly regulated permit system.
seasonNotes: Two distinct seasons: June–November is cool (18–24°C), nutrient-rich, and prime for whale sharks. December–May is warmer (24–28°C) with better viz and bigger hammerhead schools.
tripDuration: 7-night Darwin/Wolf liveaboard itineraries; combine with Galápagos land tour for a 10–14 day trip.
diveStyle: Negative entries into strong current with reef hooks; safety stops done drifting in blue water. Big-animal diving, not reef diving.
diveLevel: Advanced Open Water plus 50–100 dives is the realistic floor; most operators require it. Cold-water and current experience strongly preferred.

quotes.text: Hooked into the reef at Darwin's Arch with a wall of hammerheads above me, I forgot to breathe for a second. That's not a metaphor.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Quito or Guayaquil → Baltra (GPS). Boats embark from Baltra or San Cristóbal.

goodToKnow.title: Permit limits
goodToKnow.body: Only a handful of vessels are permitted at Darwin/Wolf. Book 12–18 months ahead.

goodToKnow.title: Arch collapse
goodToKnow.body: The iconic arch collapsed in 2021. The dive site is unchanged; the silhouette isn't.

goodToKnow.title: Park fee
goodToKnow.body: $200 cash on arrival + transit card. Bring USD.

extendedDescription: Wolf is Darwin's sibling and the other half of every northern Galápagos itinerary — hammerheads, Galápagos sharks, dolphins and the occasional orca patrol the points, with a hard-bottom plateau that lets you hook in and watch the show. Many divers consider Wolf the more consistent of the two.
seasonNotes: Same seasons as Darwin: June–November is cool and pelagic-rich; December–May is warm with bigger hammerhead schools.
tripDuration: Visited as part of a 7-night Darwin/Wolf liveaboard.
diveStyle: Negative entries, reef hooks, blue-water safety stops. 3–4 dives per day.
diveLevel: Advanced + 50–100 logged dives.

quotes.text: If Darwin is the headline, Wolf is the album. More dives, more variety, fewer crowds in the water column.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly to Baltra (GPS) or San Cristóbal (SCY) via Guayaquil or Quito. Liveaboards depart from Puerto Ayora (Santa Cruz) — the Darwin and Wolf run is an 8–12h crossing from the main Galápagos islands.

goodToKnow.title: Combined with Darwin
goodToKnow.body: There is no Wolf-only trip. Logistics, permits and fees match Darwin.

goodToKnow.title: Bring a 7mm
goodToKnow.body: Thermoclines drop fast. A 7mm with hooded vest is the local standard.

extendedDescription: The Great Blue Hole is a 124m-deep collapsed limestone sinkhole inside Lighthouse Reef — a circular sapphire stamp in the middle of the atoll. The dive itself is a bucket-list checkbox more than a marine-life experience: stalactites at 40m, a few Caribbean reef sharks circling, and a long deco-style ascent. The surrounding atoll reefs are the real diving.
seasonNotes: April–June has the calmest seas and clearest viz. November–March can blow out the long boat ride.
tripDuration: Day-trips from Ambergris Caye or San Pedro (3–4 hours each way), or a 6–7 night Lighthouse/Turneffe liveaboard.
diveStyle: One deep dive into the sinkhole (to ~40m) plus 2 reef dives on the surrounding atoll. Long boat day.
diveLevel: Advanced Open Water required by most operators; deep diver specialty preferred.

quotes.text: The Blue Hole itself is more impressive from the air than underwater — but the wall dives on the way home are quietly some of the best Caribbean diving I've ever done.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly to Philip Goldson International (BZE) in Belize City, or direct to San Pedro (SPR) on Ambergris Caye. Day-trip boats depart Ambergris Caye and Caye Caulker early morning — the round trip is 5–8h including the 2–4h crossing each way.

goodToKnow.title: Long boat ride
goodToKnow.body: 2.5–4h each way from Ambergris. Take Bonine the night before.

goodToKnow.title: Manage expectations
goodToKnow.body: Visibility inside the hole isn't always the postcard. Go for the geology, not the fish.

extendedDescription: Cocos Island sits 550km off the Pacific coast of Costa Rica — a rainforested rock surrounded by deep water and one of the planet's great shark aggregations. Schooling scalloped hammerheads, Galápagos sharks, silkies, tigers (occasional), tuna, marbled rays and resident dolphins. Liveaboard-only, with a 30–36 hour crossing each way.
seasonNotes: Year-round, but June–November (rainy season) delivers the largest hammerhead schools and worst surface conditions. December–May is calmer with clearer water and more whale sharks.
tripDuration: 10–12 night liveaboard, including 2 days of transit each way.
diveStyle: Negative entries into strong current, reef hooks, blue-water safety stops. Big-animal diving, deep cleaning stations.
diveLevel: Advanced + 50+ dives is operator-required; nitrox certification strongly recommended.

quotes.text: Two days at sea, then four days of the heaviest shark traffic I've ever seen, then two days back. Worth every hour.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly to San José (SJO). Liveaboards depart from Puntarenas on Costa Rica's Pacific coast, roughly a 2h bus or shuttle from SJO. The ocean crossing to Cocos is 32–36h each way.

goodToKnow.title: Crossing
goodToKnow.body: 32–36h each way from Puntarenas. Bring scopolamine.

goodToKnow.title: Park fee
goodToKnow.body: ~$500 conservation fee on top of trip cost.

goodToKnow.title: No land diving
goodToKnow.body: There's no infrastructure on the island for divers — it's a national park visit only.

extendedDescription: The Revillagigedo Islands — Socorro, San Benedicto, Roca Partida — sit 400km off Baja in deep Pacific water. The draw is giant oceanic mantas that approach and circle divers, plus humpback whales (in season), silky and Galápagos sharks, and the occasional bait-ball spectacle.
seasonNotes: November–May is the season. January–March brings humpback whales and their songs underwater. April–May has the largest baitfish action.
tripDuration: 8–10 night liveaboard from Cabo San Lucas, including 24h crossing each way.
diveStyle: Blue-water diving at seamounts with strong current and surge. Mantas come to you; you stay still and breathe quietly.
diveLevel: Advanced + 50+ dives. Some operators require 100.

quotes.text: An oceanic manta hovered six inches over my regulator for forty seconds making eye contact. I still don't have words for it.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly into Cabo San Lucas (SJD). Boats depart Cabo Marina.

goodToKnow.title: Park fee
goodToKnow.body: ~$50/day biosphere reserve fee.

goodToKnow.title: Crossing
goodToKnow.body: 20–28h each way. Pacific can be rough — pre-medicate.

extendedDescription: Silfra is a fissure in Iceland's Thingvellir National Park where the North American and Eurasian tectonic plates pull apart. The water is glacial meltwater filtered through 50km of lava rock — so clear that visibility regularly exceeds 100m. It's a geological dive, not a marine-life one.
seasonNotes: Year-round. Summer (June–August) is most pleasant on the surface; winter offers shorter days but the same crystal water.
tripDuration: Half-day or day trip from Reykjavík; most divers fit Silfra into a longer Iceland itinerary.
diveStyle: Drysuit only. Easy current, shallow (max 18m), short (30–40 min) dives.
diveLevel: Dry Suit Diver certification required by operators, plus 10+ drysuit dives logged. No drysuit experience? Many ops offer snorkel-only.

quotes.text: I've seen 30m visibility plenty of times. Silfra is the only place I've ever felt like there was no water in front of me.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly to Reykjavík (KEF). Þingvellir National Park (where Silfra is) is 45 minutes by car from Reykjavík — most operators run shuttle transfers. Book early; daily diver slots are strictly capped by the park.

goodToKnow.title: Drysuit cert mandatory
goodToKnow.body: Bring your card and log. Operators will turn you away without it.

goodToKnow.title: Water is 2–4°C
goodToKnow.body: Hands and face will be cold. Bring a balaclava under your hood.

goodToKnow.title: It's a national park
goodToKnow.body: Strict no-touch rules and limited slots. Book ahead.

extendedDescription: Two tiny rocks 60km offshore in the Egyptian Red Sea, the Brothers are two seamounts wrapped in soft coral and patrolled by oceanic whitetips, threshers, hammerheads, and the occasional silky. Liveaboard-only.
seasonNotes: May–November is the open season; the park closes in winter. October–November is peak for oceanic whitetips and threshers.
tripDuration: 7-night liveaboard combining Brothers/Daedalus/Elphinstone, departing Port Ghalib or Hurghada.
diveStyle: Wall and seamount diving with strong current; negative entries.
diveLevel: Advanced + 50+ dives; nitrox strongly recommended.

quotes.text: Oceanic whitetips at the Brothers don't keep their distance — they come check you out. It's the most respect I've ever felt for a shark.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly to Hurghada (HRG) — well-served from Europe. Liveaboards depart Hurghada Marina; the crossing to the Brothers Islands is approximately 5–8h overnight. Day-trip access is not permitted.

goodToKnow.title: Park closure
goodToKnow.body: Closed roughly December–April. Confirm dates with your operator.

goodToKnow.title: Park fees
goodToKnow.body: Marine park + Brothers tags add ~$80–120 on top of trip price.

extendedDescription: Ras Mohammed is the southern tip of the Sinai Peninsula, where the Red Sea's two main basins meet and pelagic traffic concentrates against dramatic walls. Day-trips from Sharm el-Sheikh deliver consistent visibility, big jacks, schooling barracuda, and the SS Thistlegorm wreck nearby.
seasonNotes: March–November is warm and calm. June–August is hot above water but the diving doesn't slow. Year-round divable.
tripDuration: 5–7 night land-based stay in Sharm or Dahab; liveaboards combine with Tiran/Thistlegorm/Brothers.
diveStyle: Mostly drift along the walls; current ranges from mild to strong at Shark and Yolanda Reefs.
diveLevel: Open Water; Advanced helps for the deeper wall and the Thistlegorm.

quotes.text: Shark Reef at slack-to-flood with the schooling barracuda overhead is one of the great 20-minute windows in diving.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly to Sharm el-Sheikh (SSH) direct from most European hubs.

goodToKnow.title: Park fees
goodToKnow.body: Daily park fee (~$10–15) collected by op.

extendedDescription: A small island off the northern tip of Cebu, Malapascua is built around one thing: pre-dawn dives at Monad Shoal, the most reliable thresher shark encounter in the world. Beyond threshers, the surrounding reefs offer macro, manta cleaning stations, and a relaxed island base.
seasonNotes: October–May is the main season. June–September has occasional typhoons but the threshers remain. Threshers are essentially year-round.
tripDuration: 3–5 nights is typical; longer if combining with Moalboal or Bohol.
diveStyle: Early-morning deep dive (30m) at Monad for threshers, then 2–3 dives a day on shallower reefs.
diveLevel: Advanced Open Water required for Monad Shoal (30m depth).

quotes.text: I set an alarm for 4:30am for a week and never once regretted it. The threshers showed up every single day.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Cebu (CEB), then 4h van + 30 min boat. Or arrange direct transfer through your resort.

goodToKnow.title: Early starts
goodToKnow.body: Dives leave around 5am. Plan your sleep accordingly.

extendedDescription: Fakarava's South Pass is the wall of sharks — 700+ grey reef sharks aggregate in the narrow channel, drifting in formation in the current. Add schooling marbled groupers (June–July spawning), Napoleon wrasse, and clear lagoon water and you have one of the planet's elite drift dives.
seasonNotes: Year-round divable; June–July is the grouper spawning + peak shark season. November–April is the wet season but still good.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights land-based at one of the pension dive lodges, or 7-night liveaboard combining Fakarava/Rangiroa.
diveStyle: Drift dives through the pass on incoming current; negative entries.
diveLevel: Advanced + comfort with current; some operators require 50+ dives.

quotes.text: Hanging in the current with 500 sharks below me was the most peaceful I've ever felt underwater.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Air Tahiti FAV from Papeete (PPT).

goodToKnow.title: Two passes
goodToKnow.body: North Pass (Garuae) is wider and easier; South Pass (Tetamanu) is the famous one. Pick a lodge accordingly.

extendedDescription: Blue Corner is a reef hook dive: you clip into the wall and watch schools of jacks, barracuda, grey reefs, and the occasional eagle ray patrol the corner where the current splits. Palau itself adds Jellyfish Lake, German Channel manta cleaning stations, and a couple of WWII wrecks.
seasonNotes: Divable year-round. November–May is drier; June–October has more rain and occasional storms.
tripDuration: 7–10 nights land-based on Koror; 7-night liveaboards available.
diveStyle: Reef hook drift diving with negative entries; current is the whole point.
diveLevel: Advanced + reef hook comfort; some sites require 50+ dives.

quotes.text: Hooked in at Blue Corner with sharks crossing every 20 seconds — there's a reason it's on every dive bucket list.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly ROR via Manila, Guam or Taipei.

goodToKnow.title: Park fee
goodToKnow.body: $100 Rock Islands permit, valid 10 days.

extendedDescription: Chuuk Lagoon (Truk) holds the largest concentration of WWII shipwrecks on Earth — 60+ Japanese vessels and aircraft sunk in Operation Hailstone in 1944, now coral-encrusted and intact. It's the world's premier recreational wreck destination.
seasonNotes: Year-round; December–April is driest. Water temp is a steady 28–29°C.
tripDuration: 7–10 night liveaboard, or land-based at Blue Lagoon Resort.
diveStyle: Wreck penetration, often deep (30–40m). Nitrox standard; many divers add rebreathers and technical training.
diveLevel: Advanced + wreck specialty for penetration; tech training recommended for deep wrecks.

quotes.text: Swimming through the engine room of the Fujikawa Maru is like time-travel with a regulator in your mouth.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: United island-hopper from Guam or Honolulu via Majuro/Pohnpei to TKK.

goodToKnow.title: Respect the war graves
goodToKnow.body: Many wrecks contain human remains. No artifact removal.

extendedDescription: A chain of nine granite islands in the Andaman Sea, the Similans deliver clear water, boulder-strewn topography, and reliable big-fish action at the northern sites (Richelieu Rock, Koh Bon, Koh Tachai). Most diving is by liveaboard from Khao Lak.
seasonNotes: Strictly mid-October to mid-May; the park is closed in monsoon season. February–April is peak for whale sharks and mantas.
tripDuration: 4–6 night liveaboard from Khao Lak.
diveStyle: Drift and boulder diving; some current at northern sites.
diveLevel: Open Water for southern sites; Advanced for Richelieu Rock and Koh Tachai.

quotes.text: Richelieu Rock in March with a whale shark cruising past purple soft coral — peak Thailand diving.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Phuket (HKT), then 1h transfer to Khao Lak.

goodToKnow.title: Park closure
goodToKnow.body: Closed approximately May 16 – October 15. Confirm dates yearly.

extendedDescription: Bonaire is the easiest world-class diving in the Caribbean — a 30km coastline of marked shore dive sites, calm leeward water, and a marine park that has been protected since 1979. Rent a pickup, load tanks in the back, and dive whatever you can find a parking spot for.
seasonNotes: Year-round; outside hurricane belt. May–September is calmest. Water 26–29°C.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights with a rental truck and unlimited shore tanks is the local rhythm.
diveStyle: Self-guided shore diving on gentle slopes; boat diving for Klein Bonaire and Washington Slagbaai.
diveLevel: Open Water; Bonaire is one of the best places to log dives independently.

quotes.text: Five dives a day for a week, no boat schedule, no waiting — Bonaire spoiled me for every other Caribbean trip.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly to Flamingo Airport (BON) — direct from Amsterdam (KLM) and several US hubs. Most dive resorts are on the leeward west coast, 10–20 minutes from the airport. Rent a pickup truck on arrival.

goodToKnow.title: Marine park tag
goodToKnow.body: $45 STINAPA tag mandatory; valid one calendar year.

goodToKnow.title: Truck culture
goodToKnow.body: Most ops include a pickup + unlimited tank rental package. Take it.

extendedDescription: Ari is the Maldives' classic dive atoll — long unbroken reefs (thilas) rising from deep blue, manta cleaning stations on the eastern side, and one of the world's most reliable whale-shark zones along the south. The diving is mostly relaxed wall and pinnacle work with the occasional ripping channel.
seasonNotes: November–April is dry season with the calmest seas and best visibility. South Ari's whale sharks are essentially year-round; mantas peak May–November on the western edge with the southwest monsoon's plankton blooms.
tripDuration: 7-night liveaboard is standard; resort-based stays run 5–10 nights.
diveStyle: Drift along thilas and channels with mild-to-moderate current; some hooked-in pinnacle dives. Liveaboard or resort dhoni boats.
diveLevel: Open Water for protected sites; Advanced helps for the channel and current dives.

quotes.text: I've snorkeled with whale sharks in three oceans and South Ari is still where I send first-timers — the odds are just better.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Malé (MLE), then seaplane or speedboat (45 min – 2h) to your resort/liveaboard.

goodToKnow.title: Alcohol rules
goodToKnow.body: The Maldives is a Muslim country — alcohol is only available on resort islands and liveaboards. Local islands are dry.

goodToKnow.title: Green tax
goodToKnow.body: $6/day per guest, collected by your accommodation.

extendedDescription: The most accessible Maldivian atoll, North Malé wraps the capital and the international airport. Channels (kandus) between the atoll's outer reefs funnel current and pelagics — grey reef sharks, eagle rays, tuna, the occasional manta — while protected lagoon thilas suit calmer diving.
seasonNotes: December–April is the dry, calm season with the clearest viz. Year-round divable but May–November brings choppier seas and reduced visibility on the western edge.
tripDuration: 4–7 nights resort-based, or as the first leg of a 7-night liveaboard.
diveStyle: Channel drifts at moderate-to-strong current; thila pinnacles and night dives in the lagoon.
diveLevel: Open Water; Advanced for channel dives.

quotes.text: First Maldives trip, easy logistics, and I still saw three grey reefs, two eagle rays and a turtle on my checkout dive.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly into Velana International (MLE) in Malé. Resort transfer is by speedboat (20–60 min) or seaplane (15–25 min). Liveaboards board directly from the marina adjacent to the airport.

goodToKnow.title: Transfer
goodToKnow.body: Speedboat from Malé to most resorts is 20–60 minutes — far cheaper than seaplane.

goodToKnow.title: Protected species
goodToKnow.body: All sharks and rays are protected nationally. Spearfishing and shark feeding are banned.

extendedDescription: Bunaken sits at the northern tip of Sulawesi in a marine park famous for its sheer wall diving — drop-offs that plunge 200m+ into deep blue, draped in soft coral and sponges, with turtles, schooling barracuda, and reef sharks patrolling the edges. Nearby Lembeh and Bangka round out a north-Sulawesi diving triangle.
seasonNotes: March–October is the main season; year-round divable. Water temp is a steady 27–29°C.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights resort-based on Bunaken or mainland Manado.
diveStyle: Wall and drift diving with mild current; easy buoyancy practice.
diveLevel: Open Water; the walls are forgiving and the marine park is well-managed.

quotes.text: Bunaken's walls feel infinite. You drift along the top and the reef just falls away below you forever.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Manado (MDC) via Jakarta or Singapore, then boat to Bunaken (45 min).

goodToKnow.title: Combine trips
goodToKnow.body: Most divers pair Bunaken with Lembeh's muck diving — opposite sides of the same peninsula.

extendedDescription: Apo Reef is the largest contiguous coral reef in the Philippines and second-largest in the world — a Marine Natural Park 40km offshore in the Mindoro Strait, accessed mostly by liveaboard. Reef shark schools, big napoleon wrasse, jacks, and pristine walls.
seasonNotes: November–May is dry season and the only viable window; June–October is closed by monsoon. March–May has the best viz and pelagic action.
tripDuration: 4–7 night liveaboard from Coron or Anilao.
diveStyle: Wall and reef-edge drift; moderate current.
diveLevel: Advanced Open Water recommended.

quotes.text: Apo is what every Philippines reef wishes it still looked like — protected, busy with sharks, and quiet on the surface.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Manila → Coron (USU) or Puerto Galera by ferry from Batangas.

goodToKnow.title: Park fee
goodToKnow.body: ~PHP 2,000 (~$35) marine park fee.

extendedDescription: Moalboal on Cebu's southwest coast is built around two things: the sardine run that lives year-round at Panagsama Beach, and the wall diving at Pescador Island just offshore. Easy shore entries, big macro on the house reef, and a relaxed backpacker town.
seasonNotes: Year-round; November–May is driest. Sardines are reliably present every month, sometimes parted by visiting thresher or whale shark.
tripDuration: 3–5 nights; often combined with Malapascua and Bohol.
diveStyle: Shore and short-boat diving on walls and the sardine ball. Mild current.
diveLevel: Open Water; great place to log dives independently.

quotes.text: Walking into the ocean from the beach and being inside a million sardines within five minutes never stops feeling absurd.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Cebu (CEB), then 3h drive to Moalboal.

goodToKnow.title: Marine sanctuary fee
goodToKnow.body: ~PHP 100/day at Pescador, collected by your op.

extendedDescription: Koh Tao is the budget-diver capital of Asia — more Open Water certifications issued here per year than anywhere else on Earth. The diving is gentle granite-boulder pinnacles in the Gulf of Thailand, with reef fish, turtles, and the occasional whale shark in season.
seasonNotes: March–October is high season with the best viz (15–25m). November–February brings monsoon rain and choppier seas but the diving still runs. Whale sharks are most likely April–June and September–October.
tripDuration: 5–10 nights for certification courses; 3–4 nights for certified divers.
diveStyle: Boat diving to pinnacles 15–30 min away; mild current.
diveLevel: Beginner-friendly; this is where most Asian divers learn.

quotes.text: I came for ten days for my Open Water and stayed three months. That's the Koh Tao story.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Bangkok → Chumphon or Surat Thani, then ferry (2–3h). Or fly Koh Samui (USM) + ferry.

goodToKnow.title: Crowds
goodToKnow.body: Major sites get busy. Ask your shop about smaller boats and off-hours trips.

extendedDescription: Richelieu Rock is a horseshoe-shaped pinnacle in the Andaman Sea that rises just to the surface — a single seamount draped in purple soft coral with whale sharks, barracuda schools, frogfish, and an extraordinary macro layer. Jacques Cousteau called it one of the world's top ten dives.
seasonNotes: October to mid-May only (Similan Marine Park is closed in monsoon). February–April is peak for whale sharks.
tripDuration: Day-trip from Khao Lak (long day) or 4–6 night Similan liveaboard.
diveStyle: Multiple loops around the pinnacle; moderate current possible.
diveLevel: Advanced Open Water recommended for the current and depth.

quotes.text: Richelieu in March with a whale shark sliding around the rock — I've chased that dive every year since.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly to Phuket (HKT) or Krabi (KBV). Day-trips depart from Khao Lak (3h north of Phuket) — the site is a 4h boat ride from Khao Lak. Liveaboards from Phuket or Khao Lak cover it as part of a Similan Islands route.

goodToKnow.title: Park closure
goodToKnow.body: Closed mid-May to mid-October. Confirm yearly.

goodToKnow.title: Liveaboard preferred
goodToKnow.body: Day-trips are 4h each way; liveaboards do it before/after the crowds.

extendedDescription: Mabul is a tiny sand island off Borneo's east coast that became one of the world's premier muck diving destinations almost by accident — divers staying nearby to access Sipadan discovered the surrounding sand and seagrass was crawling with rare critters. Frogfish, mimic octopus, mandarinfish, blue-ring octopus.
seasonNotes: April–December is calmest and clearest. Year-round divable. Water 27–30°C.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights, usually combined with Sipadan permits.
diveStyle: Slow guided macro hunts in shallow muck; very little current.
diveLevel: Open Water; buoyancy matters for the photographer-led pace.

quotes.text: Sipadan is why people come. Mabul is why they stay an extra week.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly KL → Tawau, then transfer to Semporna and boat to Mabul.

goodToKnow.title: Safety advisory
goodToKnow.body: Check current government travel advisories for east Sabah.

extendedDescription: Layang Layang is a tiny atoll in the South China Sea — a Malaysian naval station with one dive resort and one airstrip. The draw is the schooling scalloped hammerheads in deep blue water on the atoll's outside walls, plus pristine coral and few divers.
seasonNotes: Open mid-March to August only; closed the rest of the year. April–May is peak hammerhead season.
tripDuration: 5–6 nights at the single resort (Avillion Layang Layang).
diveStyle: Wall and blue-water diving with negative entries for hammerheads.
diveLevel: Advanced + comfort in deep blue water.

quotes.text: Hammerheads at Layang aren't a maybe — they're a routine 30m descent.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Charter flight from Kota Kinabalu (BKI), operated only on resort schedule.

goodToKnow.title: Book ahead
goodToKnow.body: Single small resort, short season — book 6–12 months out.

extendedDescription: The Mergui (Myeik) Archipelago is 800 islands in the Andaman Sea off southern Myanmar — barely explored, accessed only by liveaboard from Thailand, with reef shark aggregations, manta rays, and the occasional whale shark. The marine park feels untouched because it largely is.
seasonNotes: October–May only. February–April is peak for visibility and pelagics.
tripDuration: 6–8 night liveaboard from Ranong or Kawthaung.
diveStyle: Wall and pinnacle drifts; some current.
diveLevel: Advanced + 50 dives recommended.

quotes.text: We didn't see another dive boat for six days. That alone is becoming impossible elsewhere.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly to Bangkok (BKK/DMK) or Phuket (HKT), then transfer to Ranong or Kawthaung by road or domestic flight. Liveaboard operators handle the border crossing into Myanmar waters from there.

goodToKnow.title: Visa
goodToKnow.body: Most trips check you out of Thailand and into Myanmar at sea — your operator handles paperwork.

goodToKnow.title: Political situation
goodToKnow.body: Check current advisories before booking. Conditions on the ground have been unstable.

extendedDescription: Koh Rong off Cambodia's southern coast is the country's main diving spot — gentle reefs, sand bottoms, easygoing macro, and a small community of dive shops centered around the certification market. Not the world-class diving of its neighbors, but a relaxed introduction.
seasonNotes: October–May is dry season and best diving. June–September has monsoon rain and reduced viz.
tripDuration: 3–5 nights; often combined with backpacker time in Cambodia.
diveStyle: Shallow boat diving with mild current.
diveLevel: Beginner-friendly; popular for certification courses.

quotes.text: Not the most spectacular reef in Southeast Asia but the cheapest and most chill cert I've seen.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Sihanoukville (KOS) or bus from Phnom Penh, then 45-min ferry from Sihanoukville pier.

goodToKnow.title: Bioluminescence
goodToKnow.body: Many shops run free bio-luminescent plankton swims at night — worth doing.

extendedDescription: Nha Trang is Vietnam's main diving city — easy boat access to a small marine park, mostly soft coral and macro, with mild current. Recovering from years of overfishing; biodiversity is improving but not at regional-best level.
seasonNotes: March–September is best; October–February brings rough seas from the northeast monsoon.
tripDuration: 2–3 days as part of a longer Vietnam trip.
diveStyle: Boat day-trips; mild current; mostly shallow.
diveLevel: Open Water; popular for certifications.

quotes.text: Don't come to Nha Trang for the diving — come for everything else and dive while you're here.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Cam Ranh (CXR) direct from Hanoi or Saigon.

goodToKnow.title: Manage expectations
goodToKnow.body: Reefs are recovering; not in the same league as Indonesia or the Philippines.

extendedDescription: Green Island (Lyudao) off Taiwan's southeast coast has volcanic walls, hot springs that bubble up underwater, and one of the only reliable hammerhead aggregations in East Asia. The diving is dramatic and culturally different from typical tropical destinations.
seasonNotes: April–October is the main season. December–March can produce hammerhead schools but conditions are rough.
tripDuration: 3–5 nights from Taitung.
diveStyle: Wall and shore diving on volcanic topography; moderate current.
diveLevel: Advanced for the hammerhead sites; Open Water for the easier walls.

quotes.text: Diving a volcanic seafloor with hammerheads cruising at 30m and then soaking in a seaside hot spring after — only in Taiwan.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly or train to Taitung, then ferry (1h) or short flight to Green Island.

goodToKnow.title: Typhoons
goodToKnow.body: July–September has typhoon risk; build flexibility into return travel.

extendedDescription: Yonaguni is Japan's westernmost island, famous for the Yonaguni Monument — a controversial undersea rock formation that may or may not be man-made — and a winter hammerhead shark aggregation. Cold-water diving with Japanese precision.
seasonNotes: December–March is hammerhead season (and rough). April–October is calmer and warmer.
tripDuration: 3–5 nights; often combined with Okinawa.
diveStyle: Drift diving in current; some deep blue-water hammerhead descents.
diveLevel: Advanced + cold-water and current experience.

quotes.text: Whether the monument is natural or built, swimming over those right-angle terraces with hammerheads above is unforgettable.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Naha (OKA) → Yonaguni (OGN) via Ryukyu Air Commuter.

goodToKnow.title: Water temp
goodToKnow.body: 16–22°C in winter — 7mm or drysuit recommended.

extendedDescription: The Ogasawara (Bonin) Islands are 1,000km south of Tokyo — a UNESCO World Heritage natural site reached only by a 24-hour ferry from Tokyo Bay. Whale sharks, humpback whales (in season), dolphins, hammerheads, and Japanese marine life on volcanic walls.
seasonNotes: April–November is peak. Humpbacks pass through February–April. Dolphins year-round.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights, plus 48h round-trip ferry.
diveStyle: Wall and pelagic diving; some current.
diveLevel: Advanced + 50 dives.

quotes.text: The 24-hour boat ride is part of the trip. By the time you arrive, you've earned the diving.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Ogasawara Maru ferry from Takeshiba pier (Tokyo) → Chichijima. ~24h, weekly departures.

goodToKnow.title: Permits
goodToKnow.body: World Heritage site rules limit dive impact. Book through licensed local ops only.

extendedDescription: Jeju is South Korea's largest island, volcanic, and the country's only meaningful diving destination — kelp forests, lava tubes, basalt walls, and a soft-coral ecosystem unusual at this latitude. Cold water and freediving haenyeo (sea-women) culture add cultural texture.
seasonNotes: July–October is warmest (22–26°C) with best viz. Winter drops to 14–17°C.
tripDuration: 3–4 nights as part of a Korea trip.
diveStyle: Shore and boat diving on volcanic topography; mild current.
diveLevel: Open Water; Advanced helps for lava-tube sites.

quotes.text: Diving Jeju and watching the haenyeo work the same coastline by lung power was a humbling combination.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Seoul → Jeju (CJU) — one of the busiest air routes in the world.

goodToKnow.title: Water temp
goodToKnow.body: 5mm year-round; 7mm in winter.

extendedDescription: Sanya on Hainan Island is mainland China's most developed dive destination — mostly shallow reef and resort-style diving, popular for domestic certifications. Not internationally competitive, but improving.
seasonNotes: March–November; typhoon risk July–September.
tripDuration: 2–3 nights as part of a domestic China trip.
diveStyle: Shallow boat diving; very mild current.
diveLevel: Beginner; primarily a cert market.

quotes.text: If you're already in Hainan, dive. If you're picking a destination, fly somewhere else.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Sanya (SYX) direct from most Chinese cities.

goodToKnow.title: Bring your cards
goodToKnow.body: PADI/SSI cards expected; instruction is mostly in Mandarin.

extendedDescription: Havelock Island (now Swaraj Dweep) is the diving hub of India's Andamans — a remote archipelago in the Bay of Bengal with healthy reefs, manta encounters in season, and a backpacker-island atmosphere. India's best diving by a long way.
seasonNotes: December–April is the main season with the calmest seas and best viz. May–November is monsoon.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights on Havelock; longer to combine with Neil Island and Port Blair.
diveStyle: Boat diving to walls and pinnacles; mild-to-moderate current.
diveLevel: Open Water; Advanced for the deeper pinnacles.

quotes.text: India's diving secret. Healthy reefs, almost no other divers, and the food is fantastic.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Chennai or Kolkata → Port Blair (IXZ), then 2h ferry to Havelock.

goodToKnow.title: Permit
goodToKnow.body: Foreign nationals need a Restricted Area Permit, issued on arrival.

extendedDescription: Lakshadweep is India's archipelago in the Arabian Sea — a string of coral atolls more closely related to the Maldives than mainland India. Bangaram is the most accessible diving island. Permit-only entry keeps numbers low.
seasonNotes: October–May is the open season. Closed in monsoon.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights at the single resort (Bangaram Island Resort).
diveStyle: Wall and lagoon diving; mild current.
diveLevel: Open Water.

quotes.text: Maldivian water clarity at a fraction of the cost, and you almost certainly won't see another foreigner.

goodToKnow.title: Permit
goodToKnow.body: Foreign nationals need a special permit; the resort handles it.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Kochi (COK) → Agatti (AGX), then boat to Bangaram.

extendedDescription: Hikkaduwa on Sri Lanka's southwest coast is the country's longest-running dive scene — shore diving on shallow reefs, the SS Conch wreck, and macro on bommies just offshore. Diving here is the warm-up; the headline is wrecks and whales further south.
seasonNotes: November–April is dry season with the best viz on the west and south coasts.
tripDuration: 2–3 nights, usually as part of a Sri Lanka loop.
diveStyle: Shore and short-boat diving; mild current.
diveLevel: Open Water.

quotes.text: Easy diving with a beach town vibe. Combine with whales out of Mirissa and you've got a great week.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Colombo (CMB), 2h drive south.

goodToKnow.title: Whales nearby
goodToKnow.body: Mirissa (1h south) runs blue-whale tours December–April.

extendedDescription: Trincomalee on Sri Lanka's northeast coast hosts the country's best diving — Pigeon Island National Park, the British Sergeant wreck, and reliable blue whale sightings just offshore. Opposite season to Hikkaduwa.
seasonNotes: March–September is the east coast season. October–February brings the northeast monsoon and rough seas.
tripDuration: 3–5 nights.
diveStyle: Shore and boat diving on reefs and wrecks; mild current.
diveLevel: Open Water; Advanced for the wrecks at 30m+.

quotes.text: Reef sharks in Pigeon Island's lagoon, a 25m wreck in the afternoon, and dinner overlooking a temple — Sri Lanka does that.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Colombo, 5–6h drive or domestic flight to China Bay (TRR).

goodToKnow.title: Park fee
goodToKnow.body: Pigeon Island has a modest national park entry fee.

extendedDescription: Aqaba is Jordan's only coastline — a 27km stretch of Red Sea reef shared with the Saudi border and looking across at Egyptian Sinai. Shore-diveable walls, a deliberately sunk military reef (tanks, helicopters, planes), and the Cedar Pride wreck.
seasonNotes: Year-round divable; April–November is warmest. Winter water dips to 21°C.
tripDuration: 3–5 nights; often combined with Petra and Wadi Rum.
diveStyle: Shore diving; mild current.
diveLevel: Open Water.

quotes.text: An underwater Apache helicopter in 28m of blue water — Jordan does dive sites differently.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Aqaba (AQJ) direct or Amman + 4h drive south.

goodToKnow.title: Combine with Petra
goodToKnow.body: 2h drive to Petra. Plan at least 2 non-dive days.

extendedDescription: The Daymaniyat Islands off Oman's Muscat coast are the country's marine reserve — clear water, healthy reefs, seasonal whale sharks, and resident leopard sharks and turtles. Easy access from the capital.
seasonNotes: September–April is the main season. Whale sharks peak July–October on plankton blooms.
tripDuration: 3–5 nights from Muscat.
diveStyle: Boat day-trips to the islands; mild current.
diveLevel: Open Water; Advanced for some deeper sites.

quotes.text: An hour from a five-star Muscat hotel to a school of whale sharks. Unfairly easy.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Muscat (MCT) direct; dive ops on the Seeb coast 30 min from the airport.

goodToKnow.title: Park rules
goodToKnow.body: No anchoring on reef; permits managed by ops.

extendedDescription: Fujairah is the UAE's east coast on the Gulf of Oman — a 1.5h drive from Dubai and the country's only real diving. Reefs, the Inchcape wrecks, occasional whale shark visits, and an unusual east-Arabian biodiversity.
seasonNotes: October–May; summer water gets very warm and visibility drops with plankton.
tripDuration: Day-trip from Dubai or 2–3 night Fujairah stay.
diveStyle: Boat and shore diving on shallow reefs and wrecks.
diveLevel: Open Water; Advanced for the Inchcape 2 at 30m.

quotes.text: Better than any expat would expect. Whale shark season is the bonus.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: 1.5h drive from Dubai; many ops include transfer.

goodToKnow.title: Avoid summer
goodToKnow.body: June–August can hit 35°C surface water and 3m viz.

extendedDescription: Jeddah's offshore reefs are the Red Sea's quietly-best-kept secret — pristine walls and pinnacles barely touched compared to Egyptian sites further north. Saudi tourism opening has begun to make the diving accessible.
seasonNotes: October–May. Summer surface temperatures are oppressive.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights; liveaboards now emerging.
diveStyle: Wall and reef diving with moderate current.
diveLevel: Advanced recommended.

quotes.text: The reefs look like Egypt 40 years ago. The Kingdom has been protected by inaccessibility.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly into King Abdulaziz International (JED) — Saudi e-visa available for most nationalities online. Dive centres operate from the Corniche and King Abdullah Economic City areas north of Jeddah.

goodToKnow.title: Visa
goodToKnow.body: Saudi e-visa available to most nationalities since 2019.

goodToKnow.title: Dress code
goodToKnow.body: Cover-up rules on the surface; standard exposure suits underwater.

extendedDescription: Shaab Rumi is the offshore reef where Jacques Cousteau ran the Conshelf II underwater habitat experiment in 1963 — remains are still divable. Pristine walls, hammerhead schools, oceanic whitetips, and almost no other divers.
seasonNotes: March–June and October–November. Closed in summer when seas are unworkable.
tripDuration: 7-night liveaboard from Port Sudan.
diveStyle: Wall and pinnacle drift; strong current. Negative entries.
diveLevel: Advanced + 50 dives.

quotes.text: Cousteau's habitat is still there at 10m. The diving above it has barely changed in 60 years.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Most trips fly into Cairo (CAI) or fly directly to Port Sudan (PZU). Liveaboards depart Port Sudan. Check current safety advisories; some routes transit via Hurghada, Egypt, for the northern Red Sea crossing.

goodToKnow.title: Visa
goodToKnow.body: Sudan visa required; situation has been politically volatile — check current advisories.

goodToKnow.title: Logistics
goodToKnow.body: Most trips run through Egyptian operators; flight to Port Sudan.

extendedDescription: The Dahlak Archipelago off Eritrea's coast is one of the Red Sea's least-dived corners — over 100 islands, virtually no infrastructure, and reefs that haven't seen recreational divers for decades. More of an expedition than a vacation.
seasonNotes: October–May. Summer is closed practically by heat.
tripDuration: 7–10 day expedition liveaboard, infrequent departures.
diveStyle: Exploratory; unmapped sites, wall and pinnacle.
diveLevel: Advanced + 100 dives; expedition mentality required.

quotes.text: We were probably the first divers on at least three of the sites we hit. That's almost impossible to find now.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly to Asmara (ASM) via Addis Ababa or Cairo, then transfer to Massawa port (about 100 km). Expedition liveaboards depart from Massawa; most operators arrange the Massawa transfer as part of the trip.

goodToKnow.title: Visa
goodToKnow.body: Eritrean visa required and slow to obtain.

goodToKnow.title: Limited departures
goodToKnow.body: Only a handful of trips per year operate. Book through specialty operators.

extendedDescription: Djibouti's Gulf of Tadjoura hosts a seasonal whale-shark aggregation of juveniles — small enough to interact with snorkelers in shallow water. The scuba diving is secondary; the snorkel/freedive with whale sharks is the trip.
seasonNotes: October–January is whale shark season. Outside that, diving is unremarkable.
tripDuration: 5–7 night liveaboard or land-based.
diveStyle: Snorkel-first; some easy reef dives.
diveLevel: All levels for snorkel; Open Water for scuba.

quotes.text: Twelve whale sharks under a single skiff. The numbers Djibouti puts up in November are unbeatable.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Djibouti (JIB) via Addis Ababa or Dubai.

goodToKnow.title: Whale shark code
goodToKnow.body: Strict no-touch, no-flash rules. Operators enforce.

extendedDescription: Mnemba Atoll off Zanzibar's northeast coast is Tanzania's most accessible coral diving — a small protected atoll with dolphins, turtles, and reef fish in clear water, framed by Zanzibar's spice-island culture.
seasonNotes: June–October is the main dry season with the best viz. December–March is also good. Long rains April–May.
tripDuration: 3–5 nights as part of a Zanzibar trip.
diveStyle: Boat diving on the atoll reef; mild current.
diveLevel: Open Water.

quotes.text: Dive in the morning, walk Stone Town in the afternoon, that's the trip.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Zanzibar (ZNZ) via Dar es Salaam or direct from Europe.

goodToKnow.title: Marine conservation fee
goodToKnow.body: Modest fee collected at the atoll.

extendedDescription: Watamu Marine National Park on Kenya's coast offers coral diving, turtle encounters, and seasonal whale shark passes. Quieter and more locally rooted than Zanzibar, with a coastal-town feel.
seasonNotes: October–March is the main season. April–June has long rains.
tripDuration: 3–5 nights.
diveStyle: Short-boat diving on park reefs; mild current.
diveLevel: Open Water.

quotes.text: Kenya's diving is underrated and the safari add-on makes it unbeatable as a combination trip.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Mombasa (MBA) or Malindi (MYD); 1–2h drive.

goodToKnow.title: Safari combo
goodToKnow.body: Tsavo East is 3h inland — easy to bolt on.

extendedDescription: Tofo on Mozambique's southern coast is one of Africa's great big-animal destinations — manta cleaning stations, year-round whale sharks, humpback whales in season, and a relaxed beach-town base. Conditions can be challenging.
seasonNotes: Whale sharks year-round, peak October–March. Humpbacks July–October. Mantas year-round, peak May–November.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights.
diveStyle: Long boat rides through surf to deep reefs (Manta Reef, Manta Reef 2); moderate current.
diveLevel: Advanced recommended for the deeper manta sites (24–30m).

quotes.text: Two mantas circling above me and a whale shark passing on the surface, all on the same dive. Tofo does that.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Inhambane (INH) via Johannesburg, then 30 min to Tofo.

goodToKnow.title: Surf launches
goodToKnow.body: Boats launch through surf. Hold on.

extendedDescription: Aliwal Shoal off South Africa's KwaZulu-Natal coast is the country's iconic shark dive — ragged-tooth (sand tiger) sharks in winter, blacktip and tiger shark baited dives, plus the sardine run in season when it passes through.
seasonNotes: Ragged-tooth peak June–November. Sardine run typically June–July. Tiger sharks year-round.
tripDuration: 3–5 nights from Umkomaas or Scottburgh.
diveStyle: Boat diving in often-rough seas with surf launches; baited shark dives; moderate current.
diveLevel: Advanced + comfort in current and around sharks.

quotes.text: A wall of sand tigers slowly circling at 25m. Patient, calm sharks — nothing like their teeth suggest.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Durban (DUR), 1h drive south.

goodToKnow.title: Surf launches
goodToKnow.body: Inflatables launch through breaking waves; expect a wet ride.

extendedDescription: Sodwana Bay in iSimangaliso Wetland Park is South Africa's reef diving capital — a chain of named reefs (2-Mile, 5-Mile, 7-Mile, 9-Mile) accessed by surf launch, with healthy coral, manta encounters, and the occasional coelacanth at depth.
seasonNotes: Year-round divable. October–April is warmest. Whale sharks November–April.
tripDuration: 4–7 nights camping or lodge-based.
diveStyle: Surf-launch boat diving to named reefs; moderate current.
diveLevel: Open Water for nearer reefs; Advanced for 7- and 9-Mile.

quotes.text: Surf launches at dawn, four dives a day, kudu walking past camp at night — Sodwana is its own ecosystem.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Durban or Richards Bay, then 4h drive.

goodToKnow.title: Park fees
goodToKnow.body: iSimangaliso daily conservation fee.

extendedDescription: Nosy Be off Madagascar's northwest coast is the country's main diving hub — whale sharks September–December (juveniles, snorkel-friendly), reef dives on surrounding islands, and Mozambique-channel biodiversity.
seasonNotes: September–December is whale shark peak. April–November is the dry season generally.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights.
diveStyle: Boat diving to nearby islands and pinnacles; mild-to-moderate current.
diveLevel: Open Water.

quotes.text: Whale sharks every day for a week, plus humpbacks on the boat ride. Madagascar punches above its weight.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Nosy Be (NOS) via Antananarivo or direct from European hubs.

goodToKnow.title: Cyclones
goodToKnow.body: January–March cyclone risk; many ops close.

extendedDescription: Mahé is the main Seychelles island and base for the country's diving — granite-boulder topography unique to the Seychelles, big napoleon wrasse, reef sharks, and the occasional whale shark on the outer banks.
seasonNotes: Year-round; April–May and October–November are calmest. Whale sharks October–November.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights, often combined with Praslin and La Digue.
diveStyle: Boat diving on granite reefs; moderate current.
diveLevel: Open Water; Advanced for outer sites.

quotes.text: Diving among house-sized granite boulders is geologically unlike anything else in the Indian Ocean.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Mahé (SEZ) direct from Europe, Dubai, or Nairobi.

goodToKnow.title: Park fees
goodToKnow.body: Marine park fees included by ops on most sites.

extendedDescription: Praslin is the Seychelles' second island and a quieter dive base — granite topography, healthy reefs, and the legendary Vallée de Mai forest above water. Often combined with Mahé.
seasonNotes: Same as Mahé — year-round, April–May and October–November ideal.
tripDuration: 3–5 nights as part of a Seychelles loop.
diveStyle: Boat diving on granite formations; mild-to-moderate current.
diveLevel: Open Water.

quotes.text: Praslin is the more relaxed Seychelles — fewer tourists, same boulders.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Inter-island flight or ferry from Mahé (1h).

extendedDescription: Mitsamiouli on Grande Comore in the Comoros is a frontier diving destination — volcanic walls, coelacanths (in deep water, beyond recreational range), and reefs barely visited. Infrastructure is minimal.
seasonNotes: May–November is dry season. Cyclone risk December–April.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights from one of a small handful of resorts.
diveStyle: Boat diving on volcanic walls; some current.
diveLevel: Advanced recommended.

quotes.text: The Comoros is the unknown corner of the Indian Ocean. Going for the rarity, not the comfort.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Moroni (HAH) via Addis or Dar es Salaam.

goodToKnow.title: Limited infrastructure
goodToKnow.body: One or two dive ops; book ahead.

extendedDescription: Sal Island in Cape Verde sits in the central Atlantic — volcanic walls, caves, lemon sharks (in season), and the occasional whale shark and pelagic visitor. The Atlantic feel and Portuguese-creole culture differentiate it.
seasonNotes: Year-round divable. April–November is warmest. Lemon sharks December–March.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights.
diveStyle: Boat diving on volcanic topography; moderate current.
diveLevel: Open Water; Advanced for the deeper sites.

quotes.text: Lemon sharks in a sand bowl, dozens at a time. An off-the-beaten-track shark experience.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Sal (SID) direct from Europe (TAP, TUI, etc.).

goodToKnow.title: Atlantic surge
goodToKnow.body: Long-period swell on the east coast; choose sites accordingly.

extendedDescription: São Tomé sits on the equator in the Gulf of Guinea — frontier Atlantic diving with humpback whales (in season), whale sharks, and reefs barely touched by other divers. Logistics are involved; rewards are real.
seasonNotes: June–September is dry. Humpbacks July–October.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights from one of a small number of resorts.
diveStyle: Boat diving on volcanic reefs; moderate current.
diveLevel: Advanced recommended for some sites.

quotes.text: Equatorial Atlantic diving with humpbacks in the background. There's nowhere else quite like São Tomé.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly STP via Lisbon (TAP) or Accra/Libreville.

goodToKnow.title: Combine with Príncipe
goodToKnow.body: Smaller, even quieter sister island for biodiversity and turtles.

extendedDescription: The Yucatán cenotes are freshwater sinkholes connecting a vast cave system — filtered, gin-clear water, dramatic light beams, halocline (fresh-meeting-salt) layers, and otherworldly geology. Cavern diving is open-water-style; full cave penetration is technical.
seasonNotes: Year-round; rain (June–October) can affect viz briefly. Water is a constant 24–25°C.
tripDuration: Day-trips from Playa del Carmen or Tulum, or 5–7 nights to dive multiple cenotes.
diveStyle: Guided cavern diving along permanent guidelines; no current; depths 5–18m for cavern routes.
diveLevel: Open Water for cavern lines; Cave certification for further penetration.

quotes.text: Sunbeams through the entrance of Dos Ojos like cathedral glass underwater. I'll keep coming back forever.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Cancún (CUN), drive south to Playa or Tulum.

goodToKnow.title: Mask seal
goodToKnow.body: Bring a backup. Fresh water + no current means tiny leaks are noticed.

goodToKnow.title: Stay on the line
goodToKnow.body: Even in cavern zones, treat the guideline as sacred. Pull yourself along it.

extendedDescription: Turneffe Atoll is the largest of Belize's three coral atolls and home to the country's best wall diving — long uninterrupted walls, healthy reef fish, the Elbow current point, and a much quieter alternative to Ambergris Caye.
seasonNotes: April–June is calmest with best viz. June–November hurricane risk.
tripDuration: 6–7 night liveaboard or Turneffe Flats / Turneffe Island Resort stay.
diveStyle: Wall and drift diving; moderate current at the Elbow.
diveLevel: Open Water; Advanced for the Elbow.

quotes.text: Belize's Blue Hole gets the headlines, but Turneffe is the diving worth flying for.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Belize City (BZE), then boat to Turneffe (1.5–2h).

goodToKnow.title: Combine with Blue Hole
goodToKnow.body: Most Turneffe trips include the Blue Hole as a day excursion.

extendedDescription: Utila is one of the world's cheapest places to learn to dive — small Caribbean island in the Bay Islands chain, dozens of dive shops, and a reliable whale shark population on the north side. Reef diving is fine; the price and atmosphere are the draw.
seasonNotes: March–September is best for whale sharks and viz. October–February brings northers (cold fronts).
tripDuration: 5–10 nights for cert; longer for whale sharks.
diveStyle: Boat diving on shallow reefs; mild current.
diveLevel: Beginner-friendly.

quotes.text: Got my Open Water for $300, swam with two whale sharks the next week. Utila is unfair value.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Roatán (RTB) + ferry, or fly direct La Ceiba + small plane/ferry.

goodToKnow.title: Whale shark protocol
goodToKnow.body: No touching, no chasing. Ops will brief you.

extendedDescription: Roatán is the largest Bay Island — more developed than Utila, with cruise-ship traffic on the west end and quieter dive resorts on the east. Wall diving, healthy reefs, and a long-running marine park keep the reefs in decent shape.
seasonNotes: March–September is best. October–February has occasional cold-front swells.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights resort-based.
diveStyle: Wall and reef diving from short boat rides; mild current.
diveLevel: Open Water.

quotes.text: The east-end ops feel like Utila with better water and bigger walls.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly RTB direct from many US hubs.

goodToKnow.title: Pick your side
goodToKnow.body: West end is busy/cruise-heavy; east end is quieter.

extendedDescription: The Catalinas off Costa Rica's Guanacaste coast are seasonal manta and bull-shark country — volcanic pinnacles in nutrient-rich Pacific water, accessed by 30–45 min boat from Playas del Coco.
seasonNotes: May–November is peak (rainy season but best for big animals). December–April is calmer but mantas less reliable.
tripDuration: 3–5 nights from the Guanacaste coast.
diveStyle: Pinnacle and reef diving with moderate-to-strong current; some surge.
diveLevel: Advanced recommended.

quotes.text: Pacific Costa Rica isn't the prettiest water, but the manta odds in August are absurd.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Liberia (LIR), 30 min to Playas del Coco.

goodToKnow.title: Water temp swings
goodToKnow.body: Thermoclines can drop 8°C. 5mm and a hooded vest are sensible.

extendedDescription: Coiba National Park off Panama's Pacific coast is a UNESCO World Heritage site — schooling hammerheads, white-tip aggregations, mantas, and the occasional whale shark. Liveaboard or land-based, but logistics are real.
seasonNotes: December–April is dry season with calmer seas. May–November rainy with better pelagic action.
tripDuration: 5–7 night liveaboard or land-based from Santa Catalina.
diveStyle: Pinnacle and seamount diving with moderate-to-strong current.
diveLevel: Advanced + 50 dives.

quotes.text: Coiba is what Cocos was before Cocos got famous. Still empty in the water.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Panama City (PTY), 6h drive or domestic flight to Santa Catalina area.

goodToKnow.title: Park fees
goodToKnow.body: Daily park entry fee, usually included by ops.

extendedDescription: Bocas del Toro on Panama's Caribbean side is a relaxed archipelago with easy beginner diving — shallow reefs, mangrove channels, and a backpacker town vibe. Not a destination for advanced divers, but excellent for certifications.
seasonNotes: September–October and February–March are driest with best viz.
tripDuration: 3–5 nights as part of a Panama loop.
diveStyle: Shallow boat diving; mild current.
diveLevel: Beginner-friendly.

quotes.text: Not the best diving in Panama, but a wonderful place to learn and chill.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Bocas del Toro (BOC) direct from Panama City.

goodToKnow.title: Town vs. island
goodToKnow.body: Stay on outer islands if you want quiet; Bocas Town is busy.

extendedDescription: Malpelo is a UNESCO World Heritage rock 500km off Colombia's Pacific coast — schooling hammerheads, silky sharks, the rare smalltooth sand tiger, and the largest known aggregation of hammerheads in the Eastern Pacific. Liveaboard-only with a 36-hour crossing.
seasonNotes: Year-round divable; June–November is peak hammerhead season. December–May is calmer crossings with whale sharks and mantas.
tripDuration: 10–12 night liveaboard from Buenaventura, including ~36h transit each way.
diveStyle: Wall and blue-water diving with negative entries; strong current.
diveLevel: Advanced + 50–100 dives. Most ops require nitrox.

quotes.text: Pacific Colombia's Galápagos. Same hammerheads, half the divers, deeper culture.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly to Cali (CLO) or Bogotá (BOG), then connect to Buenaventura by road (2–3h) or short domestic flight. Liveaboards depart Buenaventura; the crossing to Malpelo is 36h each way.

goodToKnow.title: Crossing
goodToKnow.body: 36h each way from Buenaventura. Pre-medicate.

goodToKnow.title: Permits
goodToKnow.body: Limited vessels permitted; book 12+ months ahead.

extendedDescription: Providencia in the Colombian Caribbean is a tiny Spanish-Creole island 700km from the mainland — quiet wall and reef diving, very few other divers, and a culture distinct from anywhere else in Colombia.
seasonNotes: February–April is driest with best viz. June–November hurricane risk.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights.
diveStyle: Wall and reef diving with mild current.
diveLevel: Open Water.

quotes.text: It's a long way to get there and that's exactly why the diving still feels like the 1980s.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Bogotá → San Andrés → Providencia (small plane).

goodToKnow.title: Limited infrastructure
goodToKnow.body: Two or three dive ops on island. Book ahead.

extendedDescription: Fernando de Noronha is a Brazilian archipelago 350km off the northeast coast — clear Atlantic water, resident spinner dolphins, reef sharks, turtles, and dramatic volcanic walls. Strict environmental controls keep numbers low.
seasonNotes: August–November has the best viz (30m+). January–March is rainy with reduced viz.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights.
diveStyle: Boat diving on walls and pinnacles; moderate current.
diveLevel: Open Water; Advanced for the deeper walls.

quotes.text: Brazil's most beautiful place to dive, by a long way. Dolphins on every boat ride.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly FEN from Recife or Natal.

goodToKnow.title: Daily fees
goodToKnow.body: Environmental preservation fee + national marine park fee. Add up to $50–100/day.

extendedDescription: Arraial do Cabo on Brazil's Rio coast sits where cold upwelling current meets the tropical Atlantic — a strange biogeographic boundary with unexpected biodiversity, schools of jacks, and the country's most accessible diving from a major city.
seasonNotes: December–March is warmest; July–September has cooler water but better pelagic action.
tripDuration: 2–3 nights as part of a Rio trip.
diveStyle: Boat diving on rocky points; moderate current.
diveLevel: Open Water; Advanced for deeper sites.

quotes.text: Cold-water upwellings 2h from Rio give you fish you wouldn't expect at that latitude.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: 2.5h drive from Rio (GIG).

goodToKnow.title: Water temp swings
goodToKnow.body: Can drop from 25°C to 16°C with upwelling. Bring a 5mm.

extendedDescription: Los Roques is a Venezuelan archipelago in the Caribbean — pristine coral, healthy reefs, and unspoiled beaches. Political situation has made access intermittent over the last decade.
seasonNotes: December–April is dry season with best viz.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights at a posada.
diveStyle: Boat diving on protected reefs; mild current.
diveLevel: Open Water.

quotes.text: Caribbean diving frozen in time. The political cost has kept the reefs healthy.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Charter flight from Caracas; political situation can affect access — check current advisories.

goodToKnow.title: Cash economy
goodToKnow.body: Bring USD; cards rarely work.

extendedDescription: Westpunt on Curaçao's western tip is the island's best diving — long walls, healthy coral, quiet shore-diving access, and the Mushroom Forest. Less developed than Bonaire but with a similar shore-dive culture emerging.
seasonNotes: Year-round; outside hurricane belt. May–November is calmest.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights with rental car for shore diving.
diveStyle: Shore and short-boat wall diving; mild current.
diveLevel: Open Water.

quotes.text: Curaçao west end is what Bonaire was 15 years ago — quieter and just as good.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Curaçao (CUR), 45 min drive to Westpunt.

goodToKnow.title: Rental car
goodToKnow.body: Essential for shore diving the west end.

extendedDescription: Tiger Beach is a sand flat off Grand Bahama — the world's most reliable encounter with tiger sharks at recreational depths, often with lemon sharks and Caribbean reef sharks alongside. Baited dives in shallow, clear water.
seasonNotes: October–January is peak tiger season. Lemon sharks year-round.
tripDuration: 3–5 night liveaboard from West End, Grand Bahama.
diveStyle: Baited shark dive in 5–8m on sand; no current; spend the dive kneeling on the bottom.
diveLevel: Advanced + shark-dive comfort. Most ops require previous shark dive experience.

quotes.text: A 4m tiger shark sliding past your shoulder at arm's length, knowing she's choosing not to bite — most intense calm I've ever felt.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Freeport (FPO), boat to West End.

goodToKnow.title: Shark dive ethics
goodToKnow.body: Discussed debate; understand the baiting model before booking.

extendedDescription: The Exuma Cays are a 200km chain of Bahamian islands with clear water, swimming pigs (yes), Thunderball Grotto (Bond movie cave), and the Exuma Cays Land and Sea Park's no-take reefs.
seasonNotes: December–May has the calmest seas. Year-round divable.
tripDuration: 5–7 night liveaboard or land-based on Great Exuma.
diveStyle: Boat diving on healthy reefs and blue-hole entrances; mild current.
diveLevel: Open Water.

quotes.text: The Exumas are the Bahamas postcards come to life. Diving is the bonus.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Great Exuma (GGT) direct from US East Coast.

goodToKnow.title: Park rules
goodToKnow.body: Strict no-take in the marine park. Fines for non-compliance.

extendedDescription: Bloody Bay Wall off Little Cayman starts in 5m of water and plunges past 1,800m — one of the Caribbean's most dramatic walls, with healthy sponge and coral cover, queen angelfish, and reliable reef shark sightings.
seasonNotes: Year-round; May–October is calmest. Cayman is outside the main hurricane track but not immune.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights on Little Cayman.
diveStyle: Wall diving from short boat rides; mild current.
diveLevel: Open Water; Advanced for the deeper wall.

quotes.text: Drop in at 5m, look down, and try not to panic at the vertigo. Bloody Bay does that to first-timers.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly Grand Cayman (GCM), then small plane to Little Cayman (LYB).

goodToKnow.title: Park fee
goodToKnow.body: Marine park fee included by most ops.

extendedDescription: Stingray City on Grand Cayman's North Sound is a shallow sandbar where dozens of southern stingrays have been fed for decades by tour boats — divers and snorkelers can stand in chest-deep water surrounded by them. More photo op than dive site.
seasonNotes: Year-round.
tripDuration: Half-day trip from Grand Cayman as part of a 5–7 night stay.
diveStyle: Shallow (3–4m), no current, sandy bottom.
diveLevel: Open Water or even snorkel-only.

quotes.text: Touristy and worth it. The rays are absurdly habituated and the photos are inevitable.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Boats from West Bay or Rum Point on Grand Cayman.

goodToKnow.title: Ethics
goodToKnow.body: Decades-long feeding interaction has clear ecological tradeoffs; aware divers should understand the model.

extendedDescription: Saba is a 13-square-kilometer volcanic island in the Dutch Caribbean — the diving is on submerged pinnacles around the island, with the Saba Marine Park strictly protected since 1987. Healthy coral, sharks, and a true frontier feel above water.
seasonNotes: Year-round; May–October is calmest. Outside main hurricane track but not immune.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights at one of a handful of small dive lodges.
diveStyle: Pinnacle and wall diving with moderate current.
diveLevel: Advanced for the pinnacles.

quotes.text: Saba has the Caribbean's best-protected reefs and basically no people. The math works.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly St. Maarten (SXM), then small plane (12 minutes, Saba's airstrip is the shortest commercial runway in the world).

goodToKnow.title: Park fee
goodToKnow.body: $3/dive marine park fee.

extendedDescription: Sint Eustatius (Statia) is Saba's sister Dutch Caribbean island — historic colonial wrecks, healthy reefs, and even fewer divers than Saba. The marine park is strictly protected.
seasonNotes: Year-round; May–October is calmest.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights.
diveStyle: Wreck and reef diving on volcanic topography; mild current.
diveLevel: Open Water.

quotes.text: Statia is Saba's quieter sibling. If 'fewer divers' is your priority, you've found it.

goodToKnow.title: Getting there
goodToKnow.body: Fly St. Maarten + small plane to EUX.

goodToKnow.title: Park fee
goodToKnow.body: $6/dive marine park fee.

extendedDescription: The Bianca C is a 180m Italian cruise liner that sank off Grenada in 1961 — known as the 'Titanic of the Caribbean,' she sits in 30–50m and is one of the great big-wreck dives in the region. Grenada's broader diving includes the underwater sculpture park.
seasonNotes: Year-round; January–April calmest. Hurricane risk September–November.
tripDuration: 5–7 nights.
diveStyle: Deep wreck diving with current; reef and sculpture park as easier dives.
